data.title: Eurozone Economy In The Line Of Fire
data.description:
By Peter Vanden Houte, Chief Economist, Belgium, Luxembourg, Eurozone

War, China and tariffs

The eurozone economy is one of the most vulnerable to the Middle East war among the major economies. Although we anticipate that rising energy prices will be a temporary phenomenon, their negative impact is undeniable. At the same time, it’s important to remember that other structural challenges, such as strong competition from China and ongoing US trade tariffs, persist. And while the European Parliament has suspended ratification of the US trade deal following the Supreme Court’s move to strike down parts of Trump’s tariff package, we see little reason to expect a meaningful easing of tariffs anytime soon.

Slowing, but not halting the recovery

For Europe, higher energy prices essentially act as a foreign tax on households and businesses. Thanks to a high savings ratio, European consumers should generally be able to absorb these increased costs. However, the risk remains that diminished confidence could prompt households to save even more, rather than less.

The manufacturing sector faces renewed difficulties, having already endured higher energy costs compared to the US and China. Despite these challenges, manufacturing entered the year with some momentum, supported by relatively low inventory levels. Additionally, Germany’s fiscal stimulus is expected to gradually bolster the economy. As a result, we believe the current crisis will temporarily slow the recovery but not halt it altogether. We are forecasting weaker growth in the first half of the year, followed by a rebound in the second half, culminating in 1.1% GDP growth for 2026, after 1.5% in 2025.

Disinflation seems to be coming to an end

Higher inflation

Of course, elevated energy prices will also push headline inflation higher. Moreover, February’s HICP reading was somewhat disappointing: headline inflation rose to 1.9%, still below the 2% target, but core inflation increased to 2.4%. With energy and food prices likely to add further upward pressure in the coming months, we expect second-quarter inflation to average 2.5%, raising our annual inflation forecast to 2.1%. This is still relatively low, but it is based on the assumption that there will be only a temporary surge in energy prices. Needless to say, the risk is skewed to the upside.

ECB still on hold

Since the high inflation episode in 2022, the ECB has exercised caution in labelling inflation increases as temporary. At this point, however, we do not believe a short-lived spike in energy prices will trigger significant second-round effects. Meanwhile, calls for further rate cuts, which were widespread during the dollar’s weakening earlier this year, have likely been muted by recent events. In our view, the ECB will conclude that maintaining stable interest rates is the prudent course for the foreseeable future.

data.title: Asia's Growth Hotspots Prompt Us To Upgrade GDP Forecasts
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava, Regional Head of Research, Asia-Pacific

Recent growth performance has been strong

There are pockets of real strength across the region. India, Singapore, and Australia continue to stand out, and fourth quarter 2025 GDP prints and high‑frequency indicators reinforce that momentum. As a result, we have raised our 2026 GDP growth forecasts to 6.7% year‑on‑year for India (from 6.5%), 3.4% for Singapore (from 2.7%), and 2.3% for Australia (from 2.2%).

India’s fourth quarter GDP growth came in at a robust 7.8% year-on-year, which is impressive given that exporters were still contending with 50% US tariffs at the time. Domestic demand has remained a key engine of growth – 1Q26 indicators like motor vehicle sales point to a healthy consumption recovery, supported by easing inflation and better transmission of earlier rate cuts into borrowing conditions.

Singapore also surprised meaningfully on the upside. Its 4Q GDP growth accelerated to 6.9% YoY, powered by a strong rebound in manufacturing – especially pharmaceuticals and electronics. The city-state has been a clear beneficiary of AI‑related demand and has also gained from relatively low tariff exposure, which helped keep its exports competitive, and we expect this momentum to continue.

Australia’s fourth quarter GDP expanded by 2.6% YoY, outperforming both market expectations and the Reserve Bank of Australia’s projection of 2.3%. Growth was underpinned by solid investment activity across both the public and private sectors. Public investment continued to be supported by major transport and health infrastructure projects, while private investment benefited from strong spending on AI and technology-related initiatives. Coupled with a net positive energy trade balance, Australia remains a relative outperformer in the region amid ongoing geopolitical tensions.

The removal of elevated IEEPA tariffs is a relief for Asia

From a regional perspective, the removal of International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) tariffs represents a clear positive for Asia. China and India benefit the most, with tariff cuts of 7.1 points and 5.6 points, respectively. In their case, the new 15% rate is far better than the steep, country‑specific IEEPA tariffs they had been facing previously. It also sharply reduces Southeast Asia’s tariff burden and boosts its price competitiveness. It’s an especially important outcome for Vietnam, which relies heavily on export-led growth; this is even more important given that Vietnam is now the third-largest Asian exporter to the US.

Oil prices remain the wild card

Even without a physical supply disruption, higher global oil prices worsen trade balances and add to inflation pressures. Thailand, Korea, Vietnam, Taiwan, and the Philippines are the most exposed, with relatively higher shares of oil and gas trade deficits. It’s not just the higher oil import bill that hurts – some Asian economies could also see their export growth take a hit. India is the most exposed to the Middle East-driven export demand, with China following closely behind.

Easing cycle delayed but not over

The Iran‑driven shock to global energy prices and USD strength complicates the timing of cuts. A weaker FX profile across the region increases the risk of imported inflation and may restrain easing in the near term. We expect the upside risk to inflation from higher oil prices to delay rate cuts in Indonesia, India and the Philippines. Indonesia remains one of the few economies in Asia where the domestic demand picture still argues for monetary easing. With growth softening, Bank Indonesia (BI) retains an easing bias. While we maintain our view that BI will ultimately deliver two 25bp cuts in 2026 once currency conditions allow, we have revised the timing. Easing is no longer expected in the first quarter, with rate reductions now pushed further out in the year. Similarly, we have pushed out our final rate cut in India to the third quarter.

data.title: Archer-Daniels-Midland: Policy Wins Don't Fix This Dividend King's Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM), I reiterated their Hold rating, highlighting how the investment came with “Too Much Policy Risk For A Low-Margin Dividend King.”

Despite some improvements regarding trade and biofuel policies, following an overall expectable year, ADM remains a Hold for me, with a valuation that lacks a sufficient margin of safety and near-term macro headwinds that can weaken their balance sheet given the dividend they must pay since they want to remain a Dividend King.

Internal Developments

ADM reported mixed Q4 and 2025 results, beating the market’s Q4 profit expectations but missing on revenue, while the FCF reached a very impressive $4.2 billion despite the headwinds from biofuel policies and tariffs.

However, note that we’re talking about a massive beneficial effect from working capital, with a $1.51 billion cash inflow from the shrinking inventories combined with ~$1.06 billion from brokerage customers payables, while their adjusted FCF would reach a much more realistic ~$1.44 billion, which is not amazing for their $32.35 billion market cap, although we’re looking at macro pressure.

Still, the company is advancing on their cost savings goal, aiming at improving their efficiency, simplifying the portfolio, and delivering $500 million to $750 million in aggregate cost savings over the next 3 to 5 years, as announced last year.

ADM’s 2026 guidance sees volatile but growing adjusted EPS, reaching between $3.60 and $4.25 per share, while the adjusted net debt to adjusted EBITDA is expected to increase slightly to ~2.0x, as the company expects to ramp up their CAPEX from ~$1.25 billion in 2025 to a range between $1.3 billion and $1.5 billion, which is more in line with previous years.

Financially, based on ADM’s latest report, we continue to see one of their main advantages, with a very strong financial position where their current assets cover their current liabilities plus long-term debt - which they also reduced over the past year thanks to the benefit from inventories. This can allow them to ramp up their leverage if they want to focus on more aggressive investments, which will ultimately depend on their management.

Regarding shareholder returns, ADM is a Dividend King, with a recent ~2% increase on their quarterly dividend to $0.52 per share, well below the massive growth rates seen over the past half a decade, which I’ll continue to consider imprudent given the lack of sufficient investments, as I’ll mention below.

This represents about $1 billion following the increase, for a payout ratio of roughly 69.44% based on the adjusted FCF calculated above, which is not amazing for a ~3.09% dividend yield, plus potential buybacks that they stopped in 2025 due to weak margins.

Macro Developments

To begin with the ongoing Iran conflict, since that can have massive effects on global oil, gas, fertilizer, etc. markets, I believe there is certainly the possibility to see more issues for the global markets, especially if the end goal here is a regime change, which I believe could take even decades with American troops on the ground, without being a guaranteed win given Iran’s size, “natural fortress” environment, military capacity, etc.

As a result, we’ve already seen shipping companies avoid the entire region (including the Suez Canal) and just take the long Cape of Good Hope route around Africa, which can cause significant delays and rising costs in much more than just oil.

Even before this conflict escalated, the general consumer market was defined by weakness, very low consumer confidence (despite some improvements in February), and significant trade-downs, with big hits especially for the lower-income consumer, further enforcing the K-shaped economic gap, which can affect the demand for the more discretionary products (e.g., soda, sweets, etc.) and ultimately affect the demand for ADM’s commodities as well.

Meanwhile, this, combined with the worries surrounding Japan’s potential US Treasury sell-off, tariff uncertainty despite the Supreme Court’s decision, and others, could impact borrowing costs across the board, which can add even more pressure for ADM indirectly (weakening the environment, consumers, partners), regardless of their strong balance sheet.

Very recently, we've also seen the long-awaited EPA decision, shifting these compliance costs to larger refiners and being an overall positive for ADM, adding certainty to something that impacted their price in recent months. However, I’ll just briefly mention once again that I’d rather see better crop rotation and efficient agriculture instead of subsidy-focused, excessive corn production, as corn remains an overall bad source of ethanol/biofuels - which I highlighted in more detail here.

Ultimately, given the MAHA risks that I mentioned previously (shift away from cornstarch and ultra-processed products), ADM might turn out to be a major loser in this, as one of the country’s key “ultra-processor” that’s behind many of the companies that may be targeted, from drinks to sweets and everything else.

It’s true that they are investing and diversifying into future-facing segments such as protein (including plant-based and even insect protein), pet food, enhanced nutrition, biotics, precision fermentation, and other specialty ingredients that can help them offset these hits, but the company is investing surprisingly low amounts, in my opinion, especially now that they plan on reducing their costs.

The company's CEO also talked about the many factors affecting the demand during their Q4 Earnings Call (including even GLP-1s and their effect on sweeteners), pointing out that they can still capture the demand regardless:

There are -- we go -- when we produce -- what we produce in sweeteners, we go to so many applications, that touches many, many end uses, products and also channels. I would say, certainly, when people adopt GLP-1s, we see the consumption drops a little bit as a family. That stabilize, if you will, after 6 months, but also shift a little bit what they consume going more into proteins and maybe less savory snacks or sweet snacks. I think there is a consumer desire to move away from ultra-processed foods to a certain degree, at least initially, and I think we're seeing part of that. It is true that although inflation for food have dropped, the actual level of prices have not dropped for some of these products.

This is why I’ve been criticizing their aggressive dividend hikes, while their fundamentals didn’t keep up with this kind of growth. I would actually see a CEO change or a broader top management shift (not just the CFO following the accounting scandal) as a positive here, as I believe their investments are insufficient now, potentially leading to a rapidly narrowing gap between the dividend and the cash flow in the future, and by the time the management changes, it will be a big issue for the next guy. Either they “right-size” their investments or slow down the dividend’s growth (which we’ve seen over the past year).

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, ADM depends on the environment, and there is certainly room for things to get worse before getting any better given the broader economic weakness and the ongoing conflict in Iran.

Especially if macro weakness continues, there’s potential to see the kind of very weak dividend hikes seen recently just for the sake of increasing it to keep their Dividend King status going forward, while the stock itself could also fall as their valuation would adjust.

Valuation

For ADM's DCF to Equity valuation, given their expected normalized CFO (before working capital) and CAPEX guidance, let's use an FCF of $1.6 billion in 2026 as the base. Note that I won't adjust for net borrowings here, as I want to see their “pure” potential while assuming that the current levels of debt can be maintained going forward.

Given their CAPEX and potential for accretive investments using the stock, alongside their cost savings program and margin improvements, let’s use a 5% CAGR over the next half a decade, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a discount rate of 9% despite the volatility they’re subject to given the industry, with 484 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $27.63 billion, for an intrinsic value of $57.09 per share, which is below the current $66.37.

Conclusions

I reiterate ADM’s Hold rating, as the current valuation leaves little room for error, while their management still seems to focus more on the stock than the underlying business, although I do acknowledge that there have been some improvements lately, but not enough for their size and potential headwinds.

Even then, however, the reward is still not enough to justify taking the risk, as there are better opportunities out there in terms of yield, value, growth, and virtually anything else. Simply put, the overall risk-reward simply doesn’t justify going for ADM at today’s prices.

data.title: RVT: Expensive Small-Cap Income Fund (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

As the market continues to sell off large-cap technology companies, the rotation into other areas of the market continues. This has been beneficial for small caps and the Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT) has been able to successfully capitalize on this. When I previously covered RVT, I issued a buy rating due to the discounted price to NAV valuation at the time. Since then, RVT has provided a positive double-digit return and outpaced the S&P 500. The fund has now released an updated annual report for 2025, which prompted me to reassess the fund's value proposition and outlook for the year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RVT's share price has increased by 23%. The fund has been able to capitalize on the rotation out of large-cap tech into small-cap companies. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 34.7% over the same time frame. One of the main appeals of RVT is the starting dividend yield of 6.6%. The latest annual report indicates that the fund has no issues generating earnings that can support the payouts, which is great for investors seeking a reliable source of income.

Unfortunately, the latest price run has caused the fund to trade at a less compelling price-to-NAV valuation. Although this may no longer be the best time to accumulate shares, I see no red flags that would justify existing shareholders selling. As long as investors understand the tradeoff of prioritizing income over total return, I believe that RVT can be a solid long-term position for investors. So let's start by reviewing the underlying strategy that RVT implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, RVT now has total net assets of $2.1B that are spread across 472 different holdings. RVT's primary objective is to provide attractive total returns while providing a high current income from exposure to small-cap companies. RVT achieves this goal by focusing on companies that have a high return on invested capital, followed by strong fundamentals such as minimal debt reliance, healthy balance sheets, and earnings growth. Additionally, the fund provides a small allocation to equities outside of the U.S., which now totals ~17.3% of total assets.

The fund provides an efficient level of diversity across sectors, which helps reduce any sort of concentration risk. RVT is most heavily allocated to industrial investments at 25.6% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to financial and information technology companies, which accounts for 20.8% and 14.1% of assets. However, RVT is admittedly a bit top-heavy in these top three sectors, which could result in sensitivity during periods of market volatility. The good news is that the industrial sector tends to be more stable over time.

What I like about RVT is that each position makes up a fairly small amount of total assets since there are hundreds of holdings. For instance, the top position in IES Holdings (IESC) only makes up 2.2% of assets. RVT also provides exposure to many high quality companies, including Assured Guaranty (AGO), APi Group (APG), Arcosa (ACA), and ESAB Corporation (ESAB), just to name a few. Lastly, the fund has no fixed rebalance schedule and instead takes an actively managed approach. So RVT's holdings will change based on management's discretion through different market conditions.

Updated Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, RVT traded at a discount to NAV of 10.68%. Following the recent price run, RVT now trades at a smaller discount to NAV of 6.93%. For reference, RVT has traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.49% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that RVT now trades at the higher end of its price-to-NAV range. Therefore, the fund no longer trades at the most compelling entry point for long-term investors.

Since my last coverage, RVT has released an updated annual report for 2025. This is the most up-to-date report that provides investors with transparency into the fund's earnings and financial highlights. Net investment income amounted to $0.05 per share, while net realized gains landed at $2.85 per share. Looking back on prior years, net investment income has always been a bit weak. However, this is expected since small-cap companies aren't typically known for their ability to pay out dividends over time. The downside is that this structure means that RVT is reliant on net realized gains to fuel the majority of its growth.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions. 2025 was a strong year, so the fund was capable of locking in one of its highest levels of net realized capital gains. During sideways trending markets, the fund may be incapable of capturing a meaningful level of capital gains, which ultimately slows the rate of NAV growth. However, the fund has been able to recover from its lows of 2022, which is a sign that the strategy is still successful despite its vulnerabilities. For reference, here are the annual NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $14.26 per share
  • 2022: $10.25 per share
  • 2023: $11.72 per share
  • 2024: $12.65 per share
  • 2025: $15.24 per share

An issue I have with this reliance on net realized gains is that the fund has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions. This means that many of RVT's holdings aren't capable of growing uninterrupted over time. This ultimately limits growth potential and capital appreciation during bull markets. When assessing the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RVT's share price has increased by 59.5%. However, the inclusion of distributions takes the total return up near 250% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that the majority of their returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Interestingly, RVT has historically outperformed many standard small-cap-focused ETFs over a longer holding period. Typically, many income funds tend to underperform since they are not capable of fully participating in the upside growth of their holdings. A good example of this can be demonstrated when comparing RVT against traditional peers, including

  1. iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR)
  2. Vanguard Small-Cap Index Fund (VB)

Despite RVT's limited ability to provide capital appreciation, the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions has led to outperformance. For instance, RVT's total return over the last ten years is now approaching 250%, which severely outpaces VB's total return of 194.4% and IJR's total return of 176.9%. I believe this outperformance can be attributed to RVT's actively managed approach, which allows the fund to rotate out of underperforming positions instead of following a fixed passive approach. By cutting underperforming positions quicker compared to funds with a fixed rebalance schedule, this has proven to lead to outperformance.

Dividend Risks

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.30 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.6%. If we take this quarterly distribution and multiply it by four quarters, we get an estimated annual payout of about $1.20 per share. When referring back to the same annual report for 2025, we can see that RVT has no issues generating earnings that far exceed the distributions being paid. This is because the fund's overall yield is reasonable and doesn't try to provide payouts that are overly generous. For reference, here are the fund's annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $2.18 per share
  • 2022: ($3.87) per share
  • 2023: $1.64 per share
  • 2024: $1.35 per share
  • 2025: $2.90 per share

What I like most is that RVT is capable of generating several years worth of payouts during a single period. This excess income can help support the fund during periods where earnings aren't high enough to support payouts. This was most recently demonstrated in the 2025 period:

2025's earnings of $2.90 per share / $1.20 annual payout = 2.41 years.

This has allowed the fund to maintain a consistent rate of payouts over the last decade. However, the high-yield means that investors shouldn't have any expectations for traditional dividend growth to occur. If you are the type of investor that doesn't want to see their income decline during market pullbacks, this may not be the best choice. RVT has a history of slightly reducing payouts during market pullbacks, which is implemented to protect the NAV over time.

Despite this, I think the consistent range should still be commended because it allows investors to create their own dividend growth over time. However, the decision to focus on a high-yield fund like RVT means that it may not be the most tax-efficient choice. Despite most of the payouts being classified as long-term capital gains, there is still a sizeable portion of payouts that have less favorable tax classifications.

For instance, the latest section19(a) notice reveals that a portion of the payouts is being supported by the combination of net investment income and net realized short-term gains, which are both classified as ordinary income and have the greatest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, investors will still need to put aside some of their income to support taxes, unless RVT is being held within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on RVT to a hold at this time. The fund no longer trades at an enticing discount to NAV valuation following the recent run-up. I still believe that RVT is a great long-term choice for investors seeking exposure to small-cap companies, but this is simply not the best time for accumulation. The fund provides exposure to nearly 500 companies while providing a stable history of dividend payouts. Furthermore, the fund has a history of adjusting payouts to help preserve the NAV over time, which reinforces management's high-quality approach to portfolio management. However, investors should be aware that all of the income received from RVT may not be tax-efficient.

data.title: Kenvue: Out Of Favor Then, Under Pressure Now
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Nomura Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

In October, we sold our position in Kenvue Inc. (KVUE), a consumer health company with globally recognizable brands such as Band-Aid, Listerine, Neutrogena, Tylenol, and Johnson's baby products.

The company spun out of Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) (J&J) (JNJ) in 2023, is categorized as a household and personal care products company (HPC), and is deemed a "pure play" in consumer health given its relatively focused product portfolio. Kenvue splits its revenue into three business segments: Self Care, Skin Health and Beauty, and Essential Health. Despite significant competition within its brand categories, the company is a market leader in its three business segments globally.

In some categories, Kenvue experienced flat growth or share losses in recent years, owing in part to underinvestment in particular brands. In October 2024, activist investor Starboard Value LP announced it had built a stake in the company, believing that with proper investment, Kenvue could reinvigorate growth (specifically in the Skin Health and Beauty segment) and expand margins to 2019 levels.

At the time of our initial purchase in May 2025, Kenvue was out of favor (only 24% buy rated by Wall Street analysts) and appeared reasonably valued to us. Based on our work, the stock had a favorable long-term risk/reward profile. In September 2025, Kenvue's shares traded lower on news that the Trump administration would release a report linking autism to acetaminophen use during pregnancy. (Acetaminophen is the active ingredient in Tylenol.) The shares fell further when the administration publicly announced its recommendation that pregnant women avoid using acetaminophen unless absolutely necessary.

We believed the potential liability associated with Tylenol was limited. However, we also recognized that increasingly negative public perception could dampen overall sales. Tylenol accounts for an estimated 12-15% of Kenvue's earnings. In October 2025, news broke that a group action lawsuit had been filed against Kenvue in the UK over claims that its talc-based products cause cancer (as part of its spin-off from J&J, Kenvue assumed the company's talc-related liabilities outside the US and Canada).

The headline risks posed by the Trump administration's claims linking Tylenol to autism and the potential legal liabilities stemming from the UK lawsuit led us to believe that Kenvue's shares could remain under pressure for an extended period. In addition, we thought these issues could distract management from executing its turnaround strategy (Kenvue's turnaround was a key part of our investment thesis).

Proceeds from the sale of Kenvue were used to purchase a position in Zoetis Inc. (ZTS), a leading global provider of animal health products that was spun out of pharmaceutical maker Pfizer Inc. (PFE) in 2013.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.

data.title: EV Company News For The Month Of February 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the February 2026 edition of Electric Vehicle [EV] company news.

The past month saw a weak month for global EV sales reported for January 2026, mostly due to a 20% fall in China NEV sales and poor USA EV sales. We also heard news of slow China February NEV sales due to the Chinese New Year holiday.

Plugin electric car sales as of end January 2026

Global plugin electric car sales were 1.2 million in January 2026, down 6% on January 2025 sales. Global plugin electric car market share in January was 18% and 18% YTD (for 2026).

China plugin electric car sales were ~600,000 in January 2026, down 20% YoY. Plugin electric car market share in China for January was 39%, and 39% YTD (for 2026).

Europe plugin electric car sales were 298,000 in January 2026, up 22% YoY, reaching 30% market share and ~30% YTD (for 2026). Individual European companies market share is now updated quarterly by CleanTechnica.

USA plugin electric car sales were 86,480 (as reported by Argonne National Laboratory) in January 2026, down 25.6% on January 2025 sales. Plugin electric car market share in USA for January was 7.82%.

Australia plugin electric car sales market share was 16% for January 2026.

Brazil plugin electric car sales market share was 9.8% for January 2026.

Note: Globally, 100% battery electric cars represented ~66% market share YTD of all plug-in electric cars sold. The above sales include light commercial vehicles.

Note: An acknowledgement to CleanTechnica for their work compiling most of the electric car sales data quoted above and in the article below.

Top selling global plugin electric car groups YTD (as of end January 2026) (source)

Google Gemini summary of causes for the China sales slump in January 2026 - Reduction of China NEV subsidies was a key factor

Global EV sales forecasts in the years ahead

BloombergNEF 2026 global plugin passenger EV sales forecast - 24.3 million sales and 12% YoY growth (source)

Note: Trend Investing's forecast in 2026 is the same as BloombergNEF (see below)

Seeking Alpha's Trend Investing global electric plugin car sales forecast

Sales (million) Market share
2026 24.3 27%
2030 44 49%
2035 71 79%

Trend Investing comment

We are still only at the extremely early stage of the EV boom - We have still only electrified < 10% of the global fleet of 1.475 billion, with a staggering ~90%+ still to go.

Trend Investing's global plugin electric car sales forecast to 2040 (green bars)

EV market news

On January 28 CNEVPOST reported:

South Africa considers tariffs on vehicles from China and India. South Africa is considering imposing tariffs of up to 50% on vehicles from China and India, as policymakers said that imported goods are harming domestic manufacturing...Among Chinese brands, Chery (HKG: 9973) and GWM (HKG: 2333, SHA: 601633) maintain the largest operational presence in South Africa.

On February 4 The Maritime Executive reported:

China starts sea trials for largest electric-powered containership...The power system uses 10 containerized batteries capable of generating up to 19,000 KWh. The batteries will drive two 875 KW permanent magnet propulsion motors, and it will be possible to either recharge the batteries using high-voltage shore connections or quickly swap the batteries for charged batteries. In addition, the vessel has photovoltaic power cells to provide additional power.

World's largest electric containership (source)

data.title: Harbor International Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
We are beginning to see signs that the unprecedented global tightening cycle of the past few years is impacting companies. - Marathon London

Market in Review

The fourth quarter started with relative calm, as most global markets trended sideways until the final week of November. In the previous week, a downswing had been in place, but the trend reversed – and the upswing – comparatively gentle though it was, continued unchecked into year-end. All regional MSCI Indexes that Marathon follows ended the period ahead, with the MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) returning 3.3%. For the first time in several years, the U.S. market was a relative laggard; the regions were led by Europe, with the MSCI Europe Index generating a return of 6.2%.

The quarter's initial uncertainty seems to have been caused, at least partly, by the U.S. government shutdown, which occurred from the first day of the period until November 12. This event led to the closure of various government departments that provide economic data upon which many investors base their trading decisions. Following the resumption of services, markets began to worry that data would not be reliable enough to support their positions, and the market declined until the first official releases showed a benign picture, allowing the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut rates twice during the quarter.

By style, quality outperformed both value and growth globally. Regionally, that was only true in the U.S. In all other regions, value outperformed growth by 3%–6%. By size, large-cap stocks outperformed in all regions.

Portfolio Performance

During the fourth quarter, the Harbor International Fund (Institutional Class, "Fund") returned 2.21%, underperforming its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE (ND) Index, which returned 4.86%. We believe the Fund's underperformance was a result of stock selection in Europe.

From a sector perspective, stock selection was generally weak, and notably so in Health Care, Industrials, and Consumer Discretionary, though it was beneficial in Materials and Information Technology. Sector allocation, which is a result of stock selection rather than a separate decision, also lagged, with most positions having a slightly negative impact. From a style perspective, growth lagged value by approximately 6%, and large-caps outperformed small- and mid-caps by about 3%, respectively.

Contributors & Detractors

Leading detractors during the quarter included 3i Group (TGOPY), Roche (RHHBY), (not holding) AstraZeneca (AZN), Rolls-Royce (RYCEY), and BAE Systems (BAESY).

In November, 3i Group reported earnings in line with expectations, but warned that the outlook for key markets — particularly France, 3i's subsidiary Action's largest market — was deteriorating, which triggered a sharp sell-off; the stock fell around 25% from its previous quarterly peak.

Both Roche and AstraZeneca outperformed over the quarter; the Fund's underweight to Roche and lack of exposure to AstraZeneca was a negative for relative performance.

After performing strongly in recent years, the final quarter of 2025 saw investors reassessing their positions in defense stocks. As a result, nearly every stock in the subsector, including Rolls-Royce and BAE Systems, fell as investors took profits.

Top contributors during the quarter included Barclays (BCS), Standard Chartered (SCBFY), CaixaBank (CAIXY), Vestas Wind Systems (VWDRY), and (not holding) SAP (SAP).

Positions in the U.K.'s Barclays and Standard Chartered and Spain's CaixaBank appreciated as investors rotated into value stocks. Despite strong gains in recent years, European banks remain conservatively valued versus many non-European peers.

Denmark's Vestas, a significant player in wind turbine production, has bounced back following a period of weakness in 2024 and early 2025 due to fear of the impact of a potential, then realized, second Trump administration in the U.S. While the administration's pivot away from renewables has been notable, Vestas has been able to secure substantial orders elsewhere and reported unexpectedly strong earnings in November, beating consensus forecasts by 50%.

German multinational software company, SAP, underperformed; not holding the stock was beneficial to relative performance.

Buys & Sells

The Fund acquired Iberdrola (IBDRY), Scout24 (SCOTY), and Empresa Nacional de Telecomunicaciones (ENTIY).

Spain's Iberdrola, a leading electric utility, is diversified across the U.S., U.K., Spain, and Brazil. The firm owns natural monopoly assets in networks where new capital cannot enter due to regulation.

Scout24, Germany's leading online property portal, is a technology-led firm with recent double-digit growth. After a rally earlier in the year, shares were down 30% from their high at the time of purchase, presenting Marathon with an attractive entry point.

Empresa Nacional de Telecomunicaciones offers telecommunications services to corporate clients in Chile. Forthcoming consolidation creates scope and the opportunity for pricing power to grow.

We exited positions in Amrize, M3, and Gruma (GPAGF).

Amrize, a holding inherited from a spin-off from Swiss stock Holcim (HCMLY) in June, was sold. The firm's revenue is derived mostly from the U.S., and the stock was moved to the U.S. index in November.

M3 supplies online medical information services for doctors and supports marketing of pharmaceutical companies and medical equipment manufacturers. The stock outperformed meaningfully, and the valuation had risen significantly over the past year until the time of Marathon's exit.

Gruma is engaged primarily in the production, marketing, distribution, and sale of tortillas, corn flour, and wheat flour. The tortilla industry capital cycle appears to be deteriorating with an increasingly cost-conscious consumer down-trading to private labels from core brands like Gruma, resulting in an increasingly aggressive pricing environment.

Overweights & Underweights

The Fund's sector weightings are a by-product of Marathon London's bottom-up stock selection process and do not tend to change materially over the course of a quarter or year.

As of December 31, 2025, Industrials and Consumer Discretionary remained the largest overweights, at 5.2% and 2.4% respectively; Health Care is now the largest underweight at -4.3%, followed by Information Technology at -4.2%.

Country Allocation

The Fund's country weightings are a by-product of Marathon London's bottom-up stock selection process and do not tend to change materially over the course of a quarter or year. As of December 31, 2025, the U.K. is still the largest overweight at 12.2% followed by Denmark at 2.2%.

Outlook

Marathon London's approach is long term, with a typical target holding period of more than five years. Portfolio managers do not take short-term positions, and Marathon London does not undertake any macroeconomic research, have "house views," or make any such predictions or forecasts.

That said, one interesting observation has been the effect of the extended ultra-low interest rates on Marathon London's approach. Capital cycle analysis seeks opportunities both among high return-on-capital companies, benefiting from a positive ("top half") capital cycle, and those in a lower-return ("bottom half") universe. Usually, the bottom half offers opportunities for companies that must consolidate to improve profitability. However, there has been a dearth of opportunity in the bottom half, because very low interest rates have allowed "zombie" companies to borrow at minimal cost and continue operating. The rise in interest rates since 2021–2022 has incrementally generated some of the "creative destruction," which has been lacking in markets since the Global Financial Crisis and creates bottom-half opportunities for Marathon London. However, the lag between interest rate changes and economic impact varies and can be several years. We are beginning to see signs that the unprecedented global tightening cycle of the past few years is impacting companies. The interest cycle has turned in many countries, however, with rates being cut recently. Nevertheless, due to the lag in impact, we expect to see increased opportunity in the bottom half in the coming years.

Marathon is also cognizant of the currently heightened levels of market volatility. While our long-term approach does not seek to rapidly trade in and out of positions, it is possible that ongoing uncertainty will present opportunities to buy into assets at depressed valuations or sell holdings at high valuations.

Investment Philosophy:

The Harbor International Fund invests primarily in common and preferred stocks of foreign companies, including limited exposure to emerging market countries. The Fund seeks long-term opportunities driven by capital cycles or supply-side conditions, using a fundamental, bottom-up approach. The Fund typically invests in companies across Europe, Japan, and the Pacific Basin, with a limited allocation to emerging markets. While inherently diversified, a bias towards smaller and mid-cap businesses in niche industries, coupled with a particularly long holding period, results in a portfolio that is significantly differentiated from the Fund's benchmark index.

data.title: PayPal: Cheap For Good Reason (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In early December, I double upgraded PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) from a sell to a buy, as I believed writing the company off could have been a grave mistake. The company was gaining traction in key areas like BNPL, and the valuation seemed too depressed. As you can see in the below chart, judging by the stock's performance since then, it seems I was the one who made a mistake. In today's analysis, I will take another look at their fundamentals, recent developments, and valuation to see if I jumped the gun with my buy rating.

Below, it is shown that most of PayPal's fundamentals are deteriorating. Activity is sluggish, and the financial results are also worrying. While there is some distant hope for a turnaround with the company having replaced their CEO, soft 2026 guidance shows that things could get significantly worse from here. In hindsight, I probably underestimated the competitive pressures PayPal was facing and so believe the risk/reward is now only neutral despite a low valuation for the stock. Thus, I'm downgrading to a hold rating.

Activity Is Sluggish At Best

We'll get into their financial performance in just a little bit, but a look at some of their account and activity metrics is valuable in my view. At the end of the day, the number of people using their platform and how often they are using it don't lie. For active accounts and monthly active accounts, there was growth of 1% YoY for Q4. This suggests that PayPal's digital commerce platforms are unable to attract significant amounts of new users, and of course that isn't a great sign.

While there is some variance in performance among the transaction numbers, the 5% decline in overall transactions per active account is a sign that users are using their services less often as a whole. This is where their revenues are really generated, and so the weakness here will not bode well for their financial results. When looking at the sluggish account growth and the deteriorating TPA numbers together, there is a sense that PayPal's solutions are losing popularity somewhat, and that's obviously a potential problem.

TPV Was A Highlight

Despite the overall popularity decline, PayPal's payment services remain in a decent position. In the account metrics table above, it was shown that payment transactions grew by 2% YoY. Another reassuring sign was that TPV growth for Q4 slowed just modestly from the year-ago quarter in constant currency terms. Key areas like Venmo actually saw growth accelerate, and so despite the consumer weakness in the U.S. and in other markets, TPV has remained relatively resilient for PayPal. This should help to calm some investor fears.

Financial Performance

When looking at PayPal's Q4 financials, there are undoubtedly some signs of decelerating business momentum from what we saw in Q3. The company reported total revenues of $8.676 billion, up 3% in constant currency. That is a slowdown from 6% in the previous quarter, and so growth has become even more sluggish for the company. In addition, PayPal missed analyst expectations by a fairly large margin of $111.49 million as another sign of underperformance. Along with the top-line deceleration, transaction margin dollar growth also slowed.

Overall, the operating efficiency of the business wasn't terribly poor. Non-transaction-related expense growth of just 2% shows that they have quite good control, and you can see that operating margin contracted by a very mild 9 basis points. In my view, expense management and workforce productivity aren't the main concerns right now. The most concerning is probably the weak top-line growth that can be traced to the deteriorating overall popularity of their platforms. On the bottom line, they managed 3% of growth for adjusted EPS. This is a notable slowdown in growth from Q3 as well, but note that in percentage terms, EPS actually missed expectations by less than revenues. This confirms that top-line growth is much more worrying than the margins for the company.

Guidance Is Soft

Before going into some reasons for hope and some recent developments, let's go through some of the metrics they provided guidance for. After transaction margin dollar growth in FY2025, you can see on the right that they are expecting declines for FY2026. That implies weak performance on the top line, and so the projected operating expense growth of 3% may not be so favorable for profitability. For the bottom line, they see potential declines from $5.31 per share seen in FY2025. The analyst consensus was $5.73, and so it is clear that they guided below expectations by a significant margin. Judging by how soft their full-year outlook is, the post-earnings plunge in the stock is really not too surprising.

Strategy

While I won't go into too much depth with regard to the company's strategy, it should be mentioned that they have laid out a game plan for a comeback. This includes winning checkout, scaling Omni and growing Venmo, driving PSP profitability, and scaling next-gen growth vectors. I believe a good example of their efforts in boosting growth includes new collaborations like the recent one with TCS Blockchain.

You can read more about each of these strategic directions in their earnings presentation, but what I want to highlight here is that despite the unfavorable state of the fundamentals right now, the company still has some of the ingredients to stage a recovery. Moving forward, the quality of execution by management will likely be the determining factor on whether PayPal can turn this around or whether they will fade out of relevance.

CEO Change

On the day of their earnings, the company announced that Alex Chriss will be replaced as president and CEO by HP's (HPQ) Enrique Lores. As Liz Kiesche of Seeking Alpha writes

[Lores] led [HP] through a period of transition and innovation, expanding the business beyond its traditional PC and printing roots into services, subscriptions, and future-of-work solutions.

With the state of PayPal having deteriorated during Chriss' tenure, it makes sense for the company to bring in a new face that has what it takes to transform a business. As stated above, execution by management will be all-important moving forward, and I believe this is a good first step.

Acquirer Interest

The stock has recently seen some reprieve as there have been reports that PayPal may become an acquisition target. A deal wouldn't be too surprising with the valuation having come down immensely. The stock saw further gains after there was a report that Stripe was interested in acquiring them. However, just days later, another report stated that PayPal wasn't in any talks with any potential acquirer. At this point, with there being conflicting information regarding this topic, I wouldn't count on a deal happening, but investors should also have the possibility in mind. If they do get acquired at a premium to market valuation, obviously shareholders would benefit.

Valuation Analysis

Despite the recent uptick, the forward P/E ratio is still significantly lower than back in my previous article, when it was at a reading of 11.47. I would say that the current valuation is appropriate given the state of the fundamentals and their near-term prospects.

In my previous update, I believed the valuation of the stock was low enough for opportunity despite mixed fundamentals, with the company seemingly still a digital commerce leader. However, in hindsight, I probably underestimated the competitive pressures PayPal is facing and the potential for popularity decline in their platforms and solutions.

In today's article, it was determined that the fundamentals have deteriorated again, and their guidance suggests that the trajectory of their business may worsen in the foreseeable future. I do see some potential for a turnaround down the road, as they have laid out their strategy and have replaced their CEO. However, until there are concrete signs that a comeback is starting, I would remain cautious even if the valuation is low.

Conclusion

I will not shy away from admitting that my double upgrade of PayPal in December was likely a mistake. I believed they had all the ingredients to remain a top digital commerce company, but with their fundamentals deteriorating at a considerable pace, the valuation is cheap for a reason. As stated above, there are some reasons for hope, but they are distant, and the company has to provide proof that they can remain competitive and relevant in the rapidly evolving fintech sector. At this point, downgrading back to a more cautious and conservative rating of hold is most appropriate in my view.

data.title: Commodities: The U.S. Scrambles To Rein In Oil Prices
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy: Refined product volatility

ICE Brent settled above US$85/bbl yesterday, up 4.9%, the highest close since the Middle East conflict began. The market remains well supported with few signs of de-escalation in the Middle East and a resumption of energy flows in the region. Clearly, with every day that goes by without flows resuming, the oil market will reprice the amount of supply lost, leaving room for prices to move higher.

However, in early trading in Asia today, prices gave back some of yesterday’s gains. This follows the US's announcement that it would allow the sale of Russian oil to India for 30 days. The waiver allows the sale of Russian crude and products to India that were loaded onto vessels before 5 March and remains valid until 4 April.

This essentially allows floating Russian oil to reach India. According to Bloomberg, there are at least 9.5m barrels of Russian oil floating in Asian waters. While this might help put some immediate downward pressure on the market, it is not a game-changer. The only way for prices to come down on a sustained basis is a resumption of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

This action by the US is part of the administration's plan to try to cap oil prices. The US is also considering tapping the strategic petroleum reserve (SPR), which currently sits at 415m barrels. If the US government were comfortable drawing down the SPR to the levels seen in 2023, we could expect a release of around 68m barrels.

Refined product markets remain volatile amid disruptions in the Persian Gulf. Jet fuel cracks have seen the biggest moves amid concerns over jet fuel supply from the region. Around 23% of the global seaborne jet fuel trade moves through the Strait of Hormuz.

In addition, refiners in other regions facing disruptions in crude supply will be forced to reduce run rates. This will further tighten the jet fuel and broader refined-product markets. It is difficult to see the recently elevated levels and volatility in cracks disappear until there are signs of an imminent resumption of flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

China telling refiners to stop exporting diesel and gasoline added support to refined product cracks yesterday amid ongoing supply concerns. China exported 8m tonnes of gasoline and 6.7m tonnes of diesel in 2025.

The risk is that other countries take similar action. It would not be unreasonable to expect India to make a similar move, given its track record of taking protectionist measures amid supply concerns (though more so in food and agri markets). This would provide further upside to middle distillates.

Metals: Copper slips amid inventory surge

Copper prices softened on Thursday as a sharp rise in exchange inventories reinforced concerns that supply is outpacing demand. LME copper fell around 1.2% to settle near $12,900/t, while stocks tracked by the exchange jumped almost 8% on the day to a 16‑month high.

The inventory build reflects strong inflows into LME warehouses, driven by shifting regional pricing incentives. LME copper has been trading at only a narrow premium to Comex, reversing last year’s structure that encouraged metal to flow into US warehouses. As these pricing signals normalise, metal is increasingly being redirected back into global exchange stocks.

The inventory surge creates a tougher near‑term backdrop for prices. The key things to watch are whether LME stocks continue to build at this pace, the evolution of LME–Comex spreads, and signs of restocking from China once price pressure eases—all of which will determine whether copper can stabilise or remains vulnerable to further consolidation.

data.title: Aflac: The Richest Duck In The World
data.description:
When I was a kid, my grandmother used to give us a comic book we called Super Picsou Géant - compilations of stories starring Donald Duck, Mickey Mouse, and Uncle Scrooge (Oncle Picsou in French). That's where it started. I didn't see Scrooge as just a cartoon character. There was something else there. He felt obsessive, methodical, adventurous, fearless, and resourceful.

In the 1990s, DuckTales was on television, telling the adventures of Uncle Scrooge and his three nephews - Huey, Dewey, and Louie (Riri, Fifi, and Loulou - still in French). To me it wasn't just a children's show. It was a recurring story about wealth and patience, with a hint of adventure.

At some point - almost without realizing it - I started counting my own money. I kept a small, homemade notebook; not really a notebook, just a bunch of loose pages I had glued together myself. On those pages, I drew a chart with a ruler and a pencil, carefully plotting every new coin I earned from small jobs I did for my parents or my grandmother. Back then, it was French francs, which tells you how long ago this was.

I've since lost that little notebook, but I still remember the graph - the slow, uneven climb of savings traced in pencil. Around that same time, my father, who was a banker - and a former math teacher, explained to me the concept of compound interest. I must have been somewhere between eight and twelve years old. That lesson remained. Along with what the French call "les identités remarquables" (e.g., (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²) - simple formulas, learned early, that quietly structure everything.

Twenty years later, I am an actuary, dealing with reserves, mostly using simple approaches to forecast ultimate claim amounts. But something has remained unchanged: Scrooge McDuck remains my favorite comics character, and I still read stories written by Don Rosa and Carl Barks.

Why are you babbling about Scrooge McDuck? Because, I believe it's a fair comparison to Aflac (AFL). The Duck is not a growth story; it is a discipline story. The company operates a simple supplemental insurance model, with a dominant presence in Japan. The insurance carrier has delivered steady underwriting margins over time in both U.S. and Japanese markets. Thanks to this, the company, aware of its own limitations, has returned excess capital to shareholders through gradually increasing dividends and active share repurchases.

While demographic trends in Japan and currency exposure remain risks, the company's steady underwriting profitability and straightforward capital allocation policy support a long-term compounding profile.

Impenetrable Like A Bank Safe

Like Huey, Dewey, and Louie, Aflac's history is related to three brothers: John, Paul & Bill Amos. The company started to be a success story when the business expanded into Japan. Aflac is well-positioned in Japan, but I am not impressed by the company's commercial momentum or growth. I am impressed by its underwriting resilience. My fellow followers - and readers - know how I am focused (obsessed?) on the underwriting expertise; Aflac is not a traditional property and casualty (P&C) insurance company. Still, the benefit ratio is similar to the loss ratio, and a combined ratio can be calculated.

When I covered Aflac, in 2018, the overall over-cycle underwriting margin was about 5-7%, or a combined ratio of circa 93-95%. Since then, the underwriting performance has oscillated between 5 and 10%.

The underwriting performance for the period 2019-2024 was broadly similar to the pre-2018 period, with a lower volatility in the U.S. than in Japan. In 2025, the underwriting performance was 10.5% (8.6% in the U.S. and 12.3% in Japan)

Although the Japanese market is declining (¥1.18 trillion of annualized premiums in 2025 vs. ¥1.49 trillion in 2019 and ¥1.53 trillion in 2018), the Japanese branch remains the company's stronghold and has consistently delivered steady and recurring earnings.

Diving Into Money

What I like about Aflac is the lack of broken promises. The company's management knows the risks associated with the company's presence in Japan. The Japanese population is declining, like in South Korea, and the demographic risk is real. The size of the market won't grow, and Aflac only focuses on cancer insurance coverage and additional supplemental health coverages. Aware of its own limitations, Aflac decided to maximize the returns to shareholders.

Like Uncle Scrooge, Aflac knows what to do with every last yen. Over the last decade, the company returned about $9 billion through dividend payments.

In addition, the company repurchased massively - almost greedily - its own shares. The outstanding shares reduced from 938.6 million in 2012 to 518.7 million in 2025, or a 44.7% reduction.

Aflac is not a high double-digit grower; it is a cannibal focused on returning excess capital to shareholders.

What Could Go Wrong

Everything is not bright and perfect with Aflac. Even the shiniest duck in the world could suffer from headwinds. I see multiple risks the company faces:

  1. Yen volatility: A large share of earnings comes from Japan. A weaker yen vs. the dollar can reduce reported profits for U.S. investors. The company has tended to mitigate this risk on the investment side by increasing the exposure to the U.S. fixed-income securities. Nonetheless, the core business - the insurance book - remains dependent on yen volatility.
  2. Demographic decline in Japan: I won't reinvent the wheel, as the market is pretty aware of this risk. Japan's population is shrinking and aging. Over time this could limit the growth potential of Aflac's core market. Even worse, the market size could reduce, and the company's core business could generate lower earnings over time.
  3. Regulatory changes: One of the simplest risks to understand but one of the heaviest ones in terms of consequences. Insurance markets are highly regulated, and policyholders tend to be protected by the national regulators. Changes in pricing rules, product approvals, or capital requirements could harm the company's profitability in both markets.
  4. Execution risk in the U.S. segment: The U.S. business is historically more profitable than the Japanese segment. Still, it is a small contributor to earnings. Furthermore, the expense ratio is almost 1.5 to 1.8 times higher than in the Japanese segment. An uncontrolled increase in costs could significantly reduce the U.S segment's profitability.

Like Scrooge fighting against the Beagle Boys and Magica De Spell to protect his Money Bin, Aflac has recurring and persistent enemies: the most notable remains what I would call the "Japan risk" - which includes the foreign exchange volatility, the demographic decline, and the regulatory changes, but also the changes in consumer behavior and the competition from other insurers.

Yet in the Duck universe, Scrooge's real rivals are not thieves or sorcerers but other billionaires competing for the same title. Flintheart Glomgold and John D. Rockerduck have long challenged him for the title of the richest duck in the world. In investing, comparisons work the same way: Value is always relative.

Counting The Coins In The Money Bin

While Glomgold and Rockerduck use nasty tactics to challenge Scrooge, MetLife (MET) and Unum (UNM) are, of course, reputable companies. They do not operate in exactly the same markets, but they can be reasonably considered peers.

And much like their counterparts in the Duck Universe, they stand one or two steps behind Scrooge in terms of valuation.

The relative overvaluation versus peers is easy to explain: Aflac is simply more profitable. Although I don't like focusing on EBIT or EBITDA margins when analyzing insurers, Aflac's ROE and ROA are stronger than MET's and UNUM's.

The duck's outperformance is clearly related to its underwriting discipline and its dedication to maximizing the capital return to shareholders. The real question for investors, however, is not quality - but price.

A Great Duck, But Not A Cheap One

Aflac remains one of the most disciplined insurers in the industry. Its underwriting stability, strong Japanese franchise, and consistent capital returns have built a business that creates value over time for patient and loyal shareholders.

Yet quality rarely comes cheap. Compared with peers such as MetLife and Unum, Mr. Market assigns Aflac a valuation premium, considering it reflects the higher profitability and excess capital management.

At current prices, the stock does not appear expensive, but it also does not offer a material safety margin. For that reason, I maintain a Hold rating.

Like Scrooge McDuck carefully guarding his Money Bin, Aflac has built its wealth patiently. The price simply leaves little room for error.
